features.summary: Caterpillar reported Q4 2021 profit per share of $3.91 ($2.69 adjusted) versus $1.42 ($2.12 adjusted) in Q4 2020. Sales and revenues increased 23% to $13.8 billion driven by strong customer demand across all regions and segments. The company achieved its 2019 Investor Day targets for adjusted operating profit margins and ME&T free cash flow. However, Q4 margins were pressured by elevated freight costs and manufacturing inefficiencies due to supply chain constraints, despite the company's decision to keep plants fully operational to meet customer demand.
features.management_outlook: Management expects strong end-user demand to continue in 2022 with sales volume growth anticipated in Q1. However, supply chain constraints will limit the company's ability to fully meet demand throughout the year. Price increases are expected to more than offset manufacturing cost increases in 2022, with pricing benefits flowing evenly through the year. The company anticipates margin headwinds will be greatest in Q1 2022 but should abate as the year progresses due to easier cost comparisons and continued price realization.
features.risks: Primary risks include ongoing supply chain disruptions, particularly semiconductor shortages, which continue to constrain production and increase premium freight costs. Manufacturing inefficiencies from component shortages and elevated freight costs created unexpected margin pressure in Q4. The company faces continued inflationary pressures on materials and freight, labor availability challenges, and COVID-related uncertainties. Restructuring expenses are expected to increase significantly to $600 million in 2022 from $90 million in 2021.
features.key_drivers: Strong customer demand remains the primary growth driver across all segments, supported by robust order backlogs totaling $22.8 billion. Services revenue grew 17% to $19 billion in 2021, benefiting from digital investments and increased connectivity with over 1.2 million connected assets. The energy transition is creating opportunities through increased commodity demand for mining equipment and infrastructure investments. Government stimulus, including the U.S. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, is expected to provide tailwinds beginning late 2022.
